docs.title: pywho
docs.text:
One command to explain your Python environment.

Ever asked "Which Python am I running? Why is it using that venv? Where are my packages?" — pywho answers all of it instantly.

$ pywho

  pywho - Python Environment Inspector
  ==============================================

  Interpreter
    Executable: /Users/dev/.venv/bin/python3
    Version:    3.12.3 (CPython)
    Compiler:   Clang 15.0.0
    Architecture: 64-bit

  Virtual Environment
    Active: Yes
    Type:   uv
    Path:   /Users/dev/myproject/.venv

  Package Manager
    Detected: uv

docs.title: Why pywho?
docs.text:
• "Works on my machine" is the #1 debugging friction in Python. pywho kills it with one command.
  • Paste the output into a GitHub issue, Slack message, or Stack Overflow question.
  • Zero dependencies. Pure stdlib. Works everywhere Python runs.
  • Cross-platform. Linux, macOS, Windows. Python 3.9+.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
pip install pywho
pywho

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Interpreter details (version, compiler, architecture)
  • Virtual environment detection (venv, virtualenv, uv, conda, poetry, pipenv)
  • Package manager identification
  • Full sys.path listing
  • Site-packages locations
  • JSON output for CI/scripting
  • Installed package listing
  • Import tracing (pywho trace <module>)
  • Project-wide shadow scanning (pywho scan .)

docs.title: inspect_environment
docs.text:
from pywho import inspect_environment

report = inspect_environment(include_packages=True)

Parameters:

Name Type Default Description include_packages bool True Whether to list installed packages. Set to False for faster output.

Returns: EnvironmentReport

docs.title: EnvironmentReport
docs.text: Frozen dataclass containing the full environment snapshot.

docs.title: Interpreter fields
docs.text: Field Type Description executable str Path to the Python executable version str Python version string (e.g., "3.12.3") version_info str Major.minor.micro version implementation str Python implementation (e.g., "CPython") compiler str Compiler used to build Python architecture str "64-bit" or "32-bit" build_date str Build info string

docs.title: Platform fields
docs.text: Field Type Description platform_system str OS name (e.g., "Darwin", "Linux", "Windows") platform_release str OS release version platform_machine str Machine architecture (e.g., "arm64", "x86_64")

docs.title: Virtual environment
docs.text: Field Type Description venv VenvInfo Virtual environment details

docs.title: Path fields
docs.text: Field Type Description prefix str sys.prefix base_prefix str sys.base_prefix exec_prefix str sys.exec_prefix sys_path list[str] Copy of sys.path site_packages list[str] Active site-packages directories

docs.title: Package management
docs.text: Field Type Description package_manager str Detected package manager pip_version str \| None pip version if available packages list[PackageInfo] Installed packages (if requested)

docs.title: to_dict() -> dict
docs.text: Returns a JSON-serializable dictionary of the full report.

docs.title: VenvInfo
docs.text:
Frozen dataclass for virtual environment details.

Field Type Description is_active bool Whether a venv is active type str One of: "venv", "virtualenv", "uv", "conda", "poetry", "pipenv", "none" path str \| None Path to the virtual environment prompt str \| None Venv prompt name

docs.title: PackageInfo
docs.text:
Frozen dataclass for an installed package.

Field Type Description name str Package name version str Installed version location str Installation path

docs.title: trace_import
docs.text:
from pywho import trace_import

report = trace_import("requests", verbose=False)

Parameters:

Name Type Default Description module_name str (required) Module to trace (e.g., "requests", "os.path") verbose bool False Include all sys.path entries in search log

Returns: TraceReport

docs.title: TraceReport
docs.text:
Frozen dataclass containing the full import resolution trace.

Field Type Description module_name str The module that was traced resolved_path str \| None File path the import resolves to (None if not found) module_type ModuleType Classification: STDLIB, THIRD_PARTY, LOCAL, BUILTIN, FROZEN, NOT_FOUND is_package bool Whether the module is a package (has __init__.py) is_cached bool Whether the module was already in sys.modules submodule_of str \| None Parent module name for dotted imports (e.g., "os" for "os.path") search_log list[PathSearchEntry] sys.path entries that were checked shadows list[ShadowWarning] Detected import shadows

docs.title: to_dict() -> dict
docs.text: Returns a JSON-serializable dictionary of the full trace.

docs.title: PathSearchEntry
docs.text:
Frozen dataclass for one entry in the sys.path search log.

Field Type Description path str The sys.path entry that was checked result SearchResult FOUND, NOT_FOUND, or SKIPPED candidate str \| None File path found (if result is FOUND)

docs.title: ShadowWarning
docs.text:
Frozen dataclass for a detected import shadow.

Field Type Description shadow_path str Path of the file causing the shadow shadowed_module str Name of the module being shadowed description str Human-readable description of the shadow

docs.title: scan_path
docs.text:
from pywho import scan_path
from pathlib import Path

results = scan_path(Path("."), check_installed=True)

Parameters:

Name Type Default Description root Path (required) Directory or file to scan check_installed bool True Also check against installed packages (not just stdlib) exclude_dirs set[str] \| None None Additional directory names to skip (merged with defaults) ignore_names set[str] \| None None Additional filenames to ignore (merged with defaults)

Returns: list[ShadowResult]

docs.title: ShadowResult
docs.text:
Frozen dataclass for a detected shadow file.

Field Type Description path Path Path to the shadowing file module_name str Name of the module being shadowed shadows str What it shadows: "stdlib" or "installed:<pkg>" severity Severity HIGH (stdlib) or MEDIUM (installed) description str Human-readable description

docs.title: Severity
docs.text:
Enum for shadow severity levels.

Value Description Severity.HIGH Shadows a stdlib module Severity.MEDIUM Shadows an installed package

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text: See CHANGELOG.md for the full changelog.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: See CONTRIBUTING.md for guidelines.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.9 or later
  • No additional dependencies (stdlib only)

docs.title: With pip
docs.text: pip install pywho

docs.title: With uv
docs.text: uv pip install pywho

docs.title: Run as module
docs.text:
If you prefer not to install, you can run pywho directly:

python -m pywho

Why not uvx pywho?

uvx runs tools inside an ephemeral sandbox environment. This means pywho would report that temporary environment instead of your actual project environment. Always install pywho into the environment you want to inspect.

docs.title: From source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/AhsanSheraz/pywho.git
cd pywho
pip install .

docs.title: Development install
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/AhsanSheraz/pywho.git
cd pywho
uv venv && source .venv/bin/activate
uv pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Verify installation
docs.text: pywho --version

docs.title: Basic inspection
docs.text:
pywho

Prints a formatted report of your current Python environment.

docs.title: JSON output
docs.text:
pywho --json

Outputs a JSON object — useful for CI pipelines, scripting, or sharing in bug reports.

docs.title: Include installed packages
docs.text:
pywho --packages

Adds a full list of installed packages with versions to the output.

docs.title: Skip pip version check
docs.text:
pywho --no-pip

Skips the subprocess call to detect pip version, making the output slightly faster.

docs.title: Run as a module
docs.text: python -m pywho

docs.title: Combine flags
docs.text: pywho --json --packages

docs.title: Trace where an import resolves
docs.text:
pywho trace requests

Shows:

  • The resolved file path
  • Module type (stdlib, third-party, local, builtin)
  • Whether it's cached in sys.modules
  • The sys.path search order
  • Shadow warnings (if a local file shadows a real module)

docs.title: Full search log
docs.text:
pywho trace json --verbose

Shows every sys.path entry that was checked, not just the abbreviated version.

docs.title: JSON trace output
docs.text: pywho trace requests --json

docs.title: Shadow detection
docs.text:
If a local file shadows a stdlib or installed package, you'll see:

WARNING: './requests.py' shadows installed package 'requests'

docs.title: Scan a project for shadows
docs.text:
pywho scan .

Recursively scans for .py files that shadow stdlib or installed packages.

docs.title: Scan stdlib only (faster)
docs.text: pywho scan . --no-installed

docs.title: Scan with JSON output
docs.text: pywho scan . --json

docs.title: Scan a specific directory
docs.text: pywho scan src/

docs.title: Scan a single file
docs.text:
pywho scan math.py

Smart exclusions — these directories are automatically skipped:

  • .venv, venv, env, .env
  • __pycache__, .git, .tox, .mypy_cache
  • node_modules, dist, build, .eggs

Common non-module files are ignored: setup.py, conftest.py, manage.py, __init__.py.

docs.title: Scan in Python code
docs.text:
from pywho import scan_path
from pathlib import Path

results = scan_path(Path("."))
for r in results:
    print(f"[{r.severity.value.upper()}] {r.path} shadows {r.module_name}")

docs.title: Exit codes
docs.text: Code Meaning 0 Success (no shadows for trace / scan) 1 Shadows detected (for trace / scan) 2 Error (e.g., path does not exist for scan)

docs.title: Python library
docs.text:
from pywho import inspect_environment

report = inspect_environment()

# Access fields directly
print(report.executable)
print(report.version)
print(report.venv.is_active)
print(report.venv.type)
print(report.package_manager)

# Skip package listing for speed
report = inspect_environment(include_packages=False)

# Serialize to dict (JSON-compatible)
data = report.to_dict()

docs.title: Paste into a GitHub issue
docs.text:
pywho --json | pbcopy  # macOS
pywho --json | xclip   # Linux

docs.title: Save environment snapshot in CI
docs.text: pywho --json --packages > environment.json

docs.title: Compare two environments
docs.text:
# On machine A
pywho --json > env-a.json

# On machine B
pywho --json > env-b.json

# Diff
diff env-a.json env-b.json

docs.title: Debug a mysterious ImportError
docs.text:
pywho trace requests
# Shows exactly which file Python is loading and if it's shadowed

docs.title: Trace imports in Python code
docs.text:
from pywho import trace_import

trace = trace_import("requests")
print(trace.resolved_path)
print(trace.module_type)

if trace.shadows:
    for s in trace.shadows:
        print(f"WARNING: {s.description}")
